ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'rice cracker' class (FOODON_03302050); no specific entry for Vietnamese bánh đa nướng exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice crackers and puffed rice cakes
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers rice crackers broadly; no Vietnam-specific bánh đa nướng entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Vietnamese roasted rice cracker. No suitable parent concept identified.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Bánh Đa Nướng is one large round cracker piece weighing approximately 30 grams, commonly eaten as a snack or accompaniment to Vietnamese soups and salads.

nutrient_source.title: Rice crackers, plain — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 19016)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Vietnamese bánh đa nướng exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. USDA SR Legacy entry for plain rice crackers (NDB 19016 / FDC 168917) was used as the closest approved analogue. Bánh đa nướng is a thin roasted rice-flour wafer with sesame seeds; its macronutrient profile is consistent with plain rice crackers. Sesame seed contribution to fat and protein is minor at typical inclusion rates and is reflected in the adapted values. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: Rice cakes / rice crackers — Glycemic Index Database, University of Sydney
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Vietnamese bánh đa nướng was found. The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI values of 78–91 for plain rice cakes and rice crackers (puffed/roasted rice products). A representative GI of 82 is used, consistent with the mid-range of roasted rice cracker products in the database. Glycemic load per 30 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (82 × 24.5) / 100 ≈ 20.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~340 mg per 100 g; ~102 mg per 30 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one piece per sitting, as sodium accumulates quickly if multiple crackers are consumed, and should avoid pairing with high-sodium dipping sauces or soups.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 82) and high carbohydrate density (~81.7 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat bánh đa nướng as an occasional snack only, limiting intake to half a piece or less per occasion, and should pair it with a protein or fat source to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.6 g per 100 g) and zero cholesterol; moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Bánh đa nướng is low in saturated fat and cholesterol, making it acceptable in small quantities for individuals with cardiovascular disease, provided total sodium intake from all sources remains within the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bánh Đa Nướng is a traditional Vietnamese roasted rice cracker, widely consumed as a street snack and household staple throughout northern and central Vietnam. It is made from a thin sheet of rice flour dough mixed with sesame seeds and sometimes coconut or spices, which is sun-dried and then roasted or grilled over charcoal or an open flame until it becomes crisp, lightly charred, and aromatic. The result is a large, round, brittle wafer with a distinctive smoky, nutty flavour from the toasted sesame seeds and the roasting process. The texture is dry, crunchy, and airy. Nutritionally, bánh đa nướng is a high-carbohydrate, low-fat snack food with approximately 387 kcal per 100 g, dominated by starch from rice flour. Its glycemic index is high (approximately 82), making it a food that raises blood glucose rapidly; this is clinically significant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. Sodium content is moderate at around 340 mg per 100 g, which is relevant for hypertension management. The cracker is naturally gluten-free, vegan, and halal. It is commonly eaten on its own as a snack, crumbled over Vietnamese salads such as gỏi, or served alongside soups. In Singapore, it is available at Vietnamese restaurants as an accompaniment or appetiser.

How many calories in a Vietnamese rice cracker?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for bánh đa nướng was found in any primary regional approved source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from the USDA SR Legacy plain rice cracker entry, which is a structurally similar product. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database range for roasted rice crackers. Confidence is medium because the analogue is defensible but not an exact match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy FDC 168917 (plain rice crackers). Bánh đa nướng is a sesame-topped roasted rice wafer; the macronutrient profile is consistent with this analogue. Minor adjustments to fat and protein reflect sesame seed inclusion at approximately 5–8% by weight. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plain roasted rice crackers contain no industrially produced trans fats per USDA data). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — this is a plant-based product with no animal-derived ingredients). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no vitamin C source present in ingredients). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — vegan plant-based product). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no vitamin D source present). 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food category. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source. 4. GI of 82 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database range for roasted rice cracker products (GI 78–91). Glycemic load per 30 g serving = (82 × 24.51) / 100 ≈ 20.1. 5. The cuisine field in the input JSON was listed as 'Singapore' because the record was sourced from GrabFood Singapore; however, bánh đa nướng is a Vietnamese dish served at a Vietnamese restaurant in Singapore. Region of origin is correctly set to Vietnam. 6. Ontology codes use closest_parent match type as no exact codes exist for this specific Vietnamese product in FoodOn or FoodEx2.
